bytes.topic: Service Accounts & Roles — machine identities, workload identity federation, no long-lived keys
bytes.title: iam Service Accounts & Roles — Machine Identities with Workload Identity Federation
bytes.body: iam service accounts are used to identify machine identities, while roles define the permissions for these identities. To manage machine identities securely, iam provides workload identity federation, which eliminates the need for long-lived keys. With workload identity federation, you can configure a workload identity pool to manage service accounts and roles. This approach ensures that service accounts are authenticated and authorized without using long-lived credentials. The iam API provides methods to create and manage service accounts, roles, and workload identity pools, making it easier to implement machine identity management.
bytes.code_snippet:
gcloud iam service-accounts create my-service-account --description 'My Service Account'
gcloud iam roles create my-role --description 'My Role'
gcloud iam workload-identity-pools create my-pool --description 'My Pool'
gcloud iam service-accounts add-iam-policy-binding my-service-account --member 'serviceAccount:my-service-account@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com' --role 'projects/my-project/roles/my-role'

bytes.topic: IAM Fundamentals — identity vs principal, authentication vs authorization
bytes.title: IAM Fundamentals: Identity vs Principal, Authentication vs Authorization
bytes.body: In IAM, an identity refers to a user or entity that interacts with a system, while a principal is the entity that is granted permissions. Think of it as a user (identity) logging into a AWS IAM console (system) and being authenticated as an admin user (principal). The authentication process verifies the user's credentials, while authorization determines the permissions granted to the authenticated principal. IAM uses the concept of principals to manage access to AWS resources, such as users, roles, and AWS accounts.

bytes.topic: Least Privilege — start with no permissions, grant what's needed, regular access review
bytes.title: iam Least Privilege — Starting with Zero Permissions
bytes.body: When implementing least privilege in iam, start by creating a user with zero permissions. Then, use `UpdateUser` to grant only the necessary permissions. Regularly review access using `ListPolicyVersions` and `DetachPolicy`. This ensures that users only have the permissions required for their tasks, reducing the attack surface.

bytes.topic: Zero Trust Architecture — never trust, always verify, microsegmentation
bytes.title: iam Zero Trust Architecture — Always Verify Microsegments
bytes.body: To enforce zero trust in iam, create a microsegmented architecture by defining granular IAM policies. Use iam's Conditions feature to specify IP ranges, user attributes, and resource properties. For example, allow access to a specific S3 bucket only when the request originates from a trusted IP range: `{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "s3:GetObject", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::example-bucket/*", "Condition": { "IpAddress": { "aws:SourceIp": [ "192.0.2.1/32" ] } } } ] }`

bytes.topic: Secrets Rotation — automated rotation, zero-downtime rotation, audit trails
bytes.title: IAM Secrets Rotation: Automated, Zero-Downtime, and Audit Trails
bytes.body: To automate secrets rotation in IAM, use AWS Secrets Manager's `rotate` method and IAM roles for secrets management. For zero-downtime rotation, update your application's secrets in batches using IAM's `update-assume-role-policy` API. Audit trails can be enabled by configuring IAM's `CloudWatch Logs` to log all API calls. Use IAM roles with the `iam:CreateServiceLinkedRole` permission to create service-linked roles for secrets management.

bytes.topic: IAM Policies — allow vs deny, condition keys, policy evaluation logic
bytes.title: IAM Policies — allow vs deny, condition keys, policy evaluation logic
bytes.body: IAM policies use a deny-all approach, where all actions are denied by default. The allow action is used to permit specific actions. Condition keys can be used to further restrict permissions. Policy evaluation logic checks the user's identity, the resource's ARN, and the action being performed to determine if the policy allows the action. When multiple policies are attached to a resource, IAM uses a logical OR operation to evaluate permissions.

bytes.topic: Encryption — KMS, customer-managed keys, envelope encryption
bytes.title: IAM: Protecting Sensitive Data with Customer-Managed Keys and Envelope Encryption
bytes.body: When working with sensitive data in IAM, you can use customer-managed keys (CMKs) to encrypt and decrypt resources. CMKs are AWS-managed keys that you create, manage, and control. You can use them to encrypt resources like Amazon S3 buckets, Amazon DynamoDB tables, and more. However, when it comes to protecting sensitive data, you may need to use envelope encryption. This involves encrypting the data at rest with a CMK, and then encrypting the CMK itself with a separate key. This adds an extra layer of security, making it more difficult for unauthorized access. To implement customer-managed keys and envelope encryption in IAM, you can use the AWS Key Management Service (KMS).

bytes.topic: Audit Logging — CloudTrail/Cloud Audit Logs, what to capture, retention
bytes.title: iam: Fine-Tuning CloudTrail for IAM Audit Logging
bytes.body: When configuring CloudTrail for IAM audit logging, consider capturing 'ReadOnlyAccess' and 'IAMUserPassReadonlyAccess' policies for sensitive resource access monitoring. Store log files in Amazon S3 for at least 12 months, as recommended by AWS. Use the 'CloudTrail S3 Bucket Policy' to restrict public access to logs. To filter logs, utilize the 'CloudTrail Event Selector' with IAM-specific query parameters like 'eventSource': ['iam.amazonaws.com'] and 'resources': ['arn:aws:iam::*:*'].

bytes.topic: Cloud Security Posture — CSPM tools, detecting misconfigurations at scale
bytes.title: IAM: Detecting Misconfigurations at Scale with IAM Cloud Security Posture
bytes.body: IAM provides a built-in feature called Cloud Security Posture, which utilizes AWS Config to track and monitor resource configurations. You can leverage AWS Config Rules to detect and remediate misconfigurations at scale. To create a Config Rule in IAM, use the `CreateConfigRule` API. The rule can be triggered by a specific resource or a service-linked role. You can also define a threshold to trigger remediation actions.

bytes.topic: Network Security — security groups, NACLs, private subnets, bastion hosts
bytes.title: iam Network Security — Securing Cloud Resources with Security Groups and NACLs
bytes.body: When securing your cloud resources with iam, use security groups to control incoming and outgoing traffic. Create a group with specific rules, such as allowing incoming HTTP traffic from a specified IP range. Associate the group with your EC2 instance or RDS database. Network ACLs (NACLs) filter network traffic at the subnet level. Create a NACL with rules, such as allowing outbound HTTP traffic, and associate it with your private subnet. Use bastion hosts to access your private subnet securely.

bytes.topic: Container Security — pod security standards, runtime security with Falco
bytes.title: iam: Enforcing Pod Security Standards with Falco
bytes.body:
To ensure container security in iam, you can leverage Falco's runtime security capabilities. Start by installing the Falco operator in your iam cluster: `oc apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/falcosecurity/falco-operator/main/deploy/operator.yaml`. Then, configure the Falco operator to monitor your pods using the `falco-config` CR: `oc apply -f - <<EOF`.
`apiVersion: falco.org/v1alpha1
kind: FalcoConfig
metadata:
  name: falco-config
spec:
  rules:  - selector: {}
    output: allow
    severity: LOW
    desc: 'allow all pods'
EOF`.
This allows Falco to monitor your pods and enforce pod security standards.
bytes.code_snippet:
oc apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: falco.org/v1alpha1
kind: FalcoConfig
metadata:
  name: falco-config
spec:
  rules:  - selector: {}
    output: allow
    severity: LOW
    desc: 'allow all pods'
EOF

bytes.topic: HashiCorp Vault — secret engines, dynamic secrets, lease TTL, auth methods
WAF — rule sets, rate limiting, bot protection, tuning false positives
Data Classification — tagging sensitive data, enforcing access based on classification
bytes.title: iam Data Classification — Enforce Access Control with Resource Tags
bytes.body: To enforce access control based on data classification in iam, create a resource tag with a key-value pair. Use the `ResourceTag` resource to define the tag. For example, create a tag named `sensitive-data` with a value of `high` to identify sensitive data. Then, use the `Policy` resource to attach this tag to a specific resource. Finally, use the `iam.getAccessAnalyzer` method to analyze access to the tagged resource and enforce access control based on the tag value.

bytes.topic: Federated Identity — SSO, SAML, OIDC, cross-account access patterns
bytes.title: iam Federated Identity — SSO, SAML, OIDC, and Cross-Account Access Patterns
bytes.body: Federated identity in iam uses Security Assertion Markup Language (SAML) 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) for single sign-on (SSO) and cross-account access. To enable SSO, create an IAM identity provider (IdP) and configure the IdP to authenticate users. iam supports both SAML 2.0 and OIDC as authentication protocols. For OIDC, use the `openid_connect_provider` resource to configure the provider and roles. To grant cross-account access, create an IAM role and attach a permission set to it. Then, grant users or groups access to the role using the `iam::Role/AttachPolicy` action.

bytes.topic: Incident Response — isolation playbooks, forensics, blast radius containment
bytes.title: IAM Incident Response — Isolation Playbooks and Blast Radius Containment
bytes.body:
When an IAM role becomes compromised, containment is key. IAM provides the `CreateServiceLinkedRole` API for isolation playbooks. Use `iam.getRolePolicy` to retrieve the current policy, then `iam.createServiceLinkedRole` to create a new, isolated role. To contain the blast radius, `iam.getRoleDependencies` identifies dependent roles, which can be isolated using `iam.createServiceLinkedRole`. For example, in Python:

import boto3
iam = boto3.client('iam')
def isolate_role(role_name):
    policy = iam.get_role_policy(role_name)['PolicyDocument']
    service_linked_role = iam.create_service_linked_role(role_name, policy)
    return service_linked_role

# Isolate a compromised role
compromised_role = 'arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CompromisedRole'
new_role = isolate_role(compromised_role)

bytes.topic: HashiCorp Vault — secret engines, dynamic secrets, lease TTL, auth methods
bytes.title: iam: Secure Dynamic Secrets with HashiCorp Vault
bytes.body: When working with iam, you often need to manage sensitive data such as database credentials or API keys. HashiCorp Vault provides a secure way to store and retrieve these secrets dynamically. iam supports Vault's secret engines, which allow you to store and retrieve secrets based on specific paths. To use Vault with iam, you'll need to create a Vault instance and configure it to use the iam authentication method. Once set up, you can use the iam SDK to retrieve secrets from Vault. For example, you can use the `lease_ttl` parameter to specify a time-to-live for the secret, after which it will be automatically rotated.

bytes.topic: WAF — rule sets, rate limiting, bot protection, tuning false positives
Compliance as Code — policy as code with OPA, cloud guardrails
Compliance as Code — policy as code with OPA, cloud guardrails
bytes.title: iam Policy as Code with OPA and Cloud Guardrails
bytes.body: iam policies are defined using the iam PolicyDocument type, but compliance requirements can make these policies lengthy and error-prone. OPA (Open Policy Agent) and cloud guardrails provide a more maintainable approach by separating policy logic into a separate file. Using OPA, you can write policies in a declarative syntax, making it easier to manage complex compliance rules. For example, you can define a policy to enforce 'iam:PassRole' permission only for specific roles. Cloud guardrails further simplify this process by auto-generating iam policies based on AWS Config rules.

bytes.topic: WAF — rule sets, rate limiting, bot protection, tuning false positives
